setup.note: single-qubit marginal rate held fixed; only correlation STRUCTURE varied

verdict: DISCRIMINATING TEST -- does scalar 'compatibility' ⟨C⟩ BEAT, or merely RELABEL, the QC field's structural (stabilizer-symmetry-alignment) metric? At FIXED marginal error rate p=0.02 and FIXED correlated fraction f=0.4, sweeping ONLY the alignment structure moves the logical error rate from 0.13252 (all-misaligned, fatal weight-2) to 0.12644 (all-aligned, logically benign) -- a structure-only gap of 0.00608 at IDENTICAL marginal rate. This reproduces the literature's fixed-marginal finding (arXiv:2506.15490, 2410.23779): correlation STRUCTURE, not magnitude, sets logical performance. The scalar 'compatibility' C=(1-f)+f*aligned_fraction tracks LER perfectly (corr(C,LER)=-0.961) -- but ONLY because C is an affine function of aligned_fraction (corr(aligned_fraction,LER)=-0.961); they are the SAME axis at different resolution. VERDICT: on real-device-shaped correlation data, the compatibility lens names the CORRECT design axis (correlation structure / compatible-vs-destructive coupling, consonant with why DFS & symmetry-aligned QEC work) -- a genuine REFRAMING/synthesis value -- but a generic scalar ⟨C⟩ does NOT beat the field's own, more specific stabilizer-symmetry-alignment metric; it is SUBSUMED by it (the QC metric specifies WHICH correlations are 'compatible': those matching stabilizer support). So: (ii) TESTABLE-BUT-DATA-LIMITED for the strong 'beats standard metrics' claim (the cross-configuration real-data test is not yet done by anyone), trending (iii) 'reduces to / is subsumed by standard structural metrics' for the scalar form. HONEST: Bucket 0 = 0; this is a toy mechanism + a verdict on the metric's status, NOT a claim of demonstrated novelty on real data.
